food_name: Curry Puff, Baked Original (Old Chang Kee-style, flaky layered pastry, potato-chicken curry filling, ~100g)
region_of_origin: Singapore (evolved from Indian samosa and British/Portuguese pastry traditions; Old Chang Kee established 1956)
food_subgroup: Baked layered shortcrust/lard pastry with curry potato-chicken filling; 1 puff ~100g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for curry puff as a prepared Singaporean hawker snack. Closest parent would be a filled savoury pastry concept; URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Filled pastry, baked, savoury (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0HAB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a baked savoury filled pastry; no exact code exists for Singapore curry puff.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for curry puff as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard baked curry puff as sold at Old Chang Kee outlets and Singapore hawker stalls, approximately 100g per piece.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Baked) — Old Chang Kee Published Nutrition Data cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore + Old Chang Kee Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for baked curry puff / karipap, cross-referenced with Old Chang Kee published nutrition information. Values represent a 100g baked flaky-pastry curry puff with potato-chicken curry filling, consistent with Old Chang Kee Original Curry Puff product specification. Minor micronutrient values (selenium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, B-vitamins) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for analogous filled savoury pastry components where HPB data was incomplete.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Potato-Containing Pastry Products — University of Sydney GI Database cross-reference; fat-moderated GI estimation for baked filled pastry with potato filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore baked curry puff in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 is estimated by analogy: plain baked potato GI ~78 (University of Sydney GI Database); fat content of the lard/shortening pastry (~13.5g fat per 100g) is well-established to suppress gastric emptying and reduce postprandial glucose response, moderating effective GI from ~78 toward ~55. This is consistent with GI values reported for other fat-enriched potato-filled pastries in the University of Sydney database (e.g., samosa-type pastries GI 55–60). GL per 100g serving = (55 × 36) / 100 = 19.8.

hypertension.guidance: One baked curry puff (~100g) contributes approximately 430mg sodium (~19% of the 2,300mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit to one puff per occasion and ensure the remainder of the day's meals are low in sodium to stay within target.

type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of ~19.8 per 100g puff, this snack produces a moderate postprandial glucose rise comparable to one cup of cooked white rice; people with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one puff per snack occasion, pair with a protein or vegetable source, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.5g per puff from lard/shortening pastry; sodium ~430mg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each baked curry puff provides approximately 5.5g saturated fat (~28% of the 20g daily limit for a 2,000 kcal diet) and 430mg sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional snack rather than a daily food, and balance with low-saturated-fat, low-sodium meals throughout the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat; culinary-dose turmeric in curry powder
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat from pastry shortening (~5.5g per puff) is moderate and unlikely to meaningfully worsen inflammation at one-puff serving frequency; culinary-dose curcumin from curry powder (~10–20mg per puff) is far below the 500–2,000mg therapeutic dose studied in RCTs and provides no clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit on its own.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Singapore baked curry puff (Old Chang Kee-style) is a flaky, layered shortcrust pastry made with lard or vegetable shortening, filled with a spiced potato-and-chicken curry mixture seasoned with curry powder, onion, and salt. Each puff weighs approximately 100g and is baked (not deep-fried), producing a golden, crisp, multi-layered shell with a moist, savoury filling. Old Chang Kee (established 1956) is the dominant branded curry puff chain in Singapore and is the benchmark for this style. The dish evolved from the Indian samosa and British/Portuguese pastry traditions and is now a quintessential Singapore hawker snack consumed as a morning or afternoon snack at hawker centres, coffee shops, and Old Chang Kee kiosks. Nutritionally, one 100g puff provides approximately 295 kcal, 7g protein, 13.5g fat (5.5g saturated from lard/shortening), 36g carbohydrate, 2.5g sugar, 2g dietary fibre, and 430mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low; fat in the pastry moderates the potato GI from ~78 to ~55), with a glycemic load of ~19.8 per puff — comparable to one cup of cooked white rice. Clinically, the main concerns are moderate sodium (430mg/puff, relevant for hypertension), moderate saturated fat (5.5g/puff, relevant for cardiovascular disease), and moderate glycemic load (relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management). Hawker stall versions are typically 20–30% larger (120–130g) with proportionally more fat and calories. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Contains gluten (wheat flour pastry) and egg.
common_user_queries: old chang kee curry puff nutrition
how many calories in a curry puff
is curry puff healthy for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) — the primary approved source for Singapore hawker foods — cross-referenced with Old Chang Kee published nutrition data. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using fat-moderated potato pastry analogues, with confidence 0.9. All values are internally consistent (energy check: 7×4 + 13.5×9 + 36×4 = 28 + 121.5 + 144 = 293.5 kcal ≈ 295 kcal reported; within rounding tolerance).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Singapore baked curry puff not available in HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for the composite product. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore data for baked curry puff; modern shortening formulations in Singapore are largely trans-fat-free. vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this plant-and-poultry-based baked pastry product with no significant vitamin D source ingredients. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate (not a direct measurement); actual GI may vary by recipe, fat content, and pastry thickness. Hawker stall versions are typically 20–30% larger (120–130g) — multiply all per-100g values by 1.2–1.3 for hawker portions. Input record description stated 'deep-fried pastry filled with beef' which conflicts with the record name (baked, chicken); the baked potato-chicken specification from the name and food_subgroup has been used as authoritative, consistent with Old Chang Kee Original Curry Puff product.
